narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and upper-middle family income, your law school application stood out in some positive ways but ultimately didn't clear the bar due to the admissions model's assessment of your bar exam prospects. The model, trained on data from thousands of past students, predicted a 41.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. This prediction hinged on a few key aspects of your profile, with your undergraduate GPA, family income, and first-year grade decile emerging as the strongest influences. Your solid 3.20 GPA—right around the typical 3.21 average for applicants—actually worked in your favor, nudging the model toward seeing you as more likely to pass the bar, much like it does for many students with comparable records. Similarly, your upper-middle family income provided a subtle boost, as the model associates this bracket with slightly better odds of bar success compared to lower incomes in the dataset. However, your first-year decile of 1—well below the average of about 5.7—pushed strongly in the opposite direction, signaling to the model a weaker early academic foundation that correlates with higher bar failure risk for students in similar positions. While your LSAT score of 26.5 is notably lower than the 36.7 average and your full-time enrollment plans align with most applicants, these played smaller roles here. Overall, your early performance dip outweighed those strengths, placing you below the threshold where the model expects reliable bar passage, unlike many peers who start stronger in their first year. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a stable background—plenty of students rebound—but the model flags patterns from past data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your first-year record through additional coursework or explanations in a new application, or targeting schools with more holistic reviews. You might also seek bar prep resources early to build confidence. With targeted improvements, your GPA and background could position you well for another shot at admission.
